title: How to Get Your Local Business Found on Google Maps
description: Google Maps is where local customers look first. Here's what actually determines whether your business shows up — and how to improve your ranking.
content:
Why Google Maps Matters More Than You Think

When someone searches "coffee shop near me" or "dentist Gdańsk", they're shown a map with three local businesses before any other results. This is called the "local pack" — and it gets more clicks than everything below it combined.

If your business isn't in that top three, you're invisible to most of those searchers. And if your business isn't on Google Maps at all, you're invisible to everyone.

The good news: ranking in Google Maps for a local business is very achievable, especially compared to competing for general web search. You're only competing with other businesses in your area — not the entire internet.

Step 1: Claim and Verify Your Google Business Profile

Google Business Profile (formerly Google My Business) is the free listing that controls how your business appears in Google Maps and local search. If you haven't claimed yours, do it now — it's free and it's the single most impactful thing you can do.

To claim your profile:

  • Search for your business on Google Maps
  • If it appears, click "Claim this business"
  • If it doesn't appear, go to business.google.com and create a new listing
  • Verify your business (usually via postcard, phone, or video call)

Once verified, fill in every single field: business name, category, address, phone, website, opening hours, description, and photos.

Step 2: Consistency is Everything (NAP)

Google checks whether your business name, address, and phone number (NAP) are consistent across the web. If your website says "ul. Marszałkowska 5" but your Facebook says "Marszałkowska 5/3", Google gets confused and trusts you less.

Make sure your NAP is identical everywhere: your website, Google Business Profile, Facebook, Instagram, and any directories like Panorama Firm or Yellow Pages.

Step 3: Get More Google Reviews

Reviews are one of the most powerful ranking factors for local search. Not just the quantity, but the recency and the way you respond to them.

The best way to get reviews: just ask. After completing a job, send a simple message: "If you're happy with our work, we'd really appreciate a Google review — it only takes a minute." Include a direct link to your review page.

Aim for at least one new review every two weeks. Respond to every review — positive and negative. This shows Google (and potential customers) that you're active and care about your customers.

Step 4: Optimize Your Website for Local Keywords

Your website and your Google Business Profile work together. Google uses signals from your website to understand what you do and where you do it.

Make sure your website clearly mentions:

  • The services you offer (using natural language customers would search)
  • The city or area you serve
  • Your physical address (ideally in the footer of every page)

For example, instead of just saying "We offer plumbing services", say "We offer emergency plumbing repairs across Warsaw and surrounding areas".

Step 5: Build Local Citations

Citations are mentions of your business on other websites — typically directories, local news sites, or industry-specific platforms. Each citation reinforces your location and legitimacy to Google.

Priority Polish directories to list your business:

  • Panorama Firm (panoramafirm.pl)
  • Yelp Poland
  • Aleo.com
  • Zumi.pl
  • Local chamber of commerce websites

How Long Does It Take?

Local SEO is not instant, but it's faster than many people expect. With a well-optimized Google Business Profile and a proper website, you can start seeing movement in local rankings within 4–8 weeks. Consistent effort — adding photos, getting reviews, updating information — compounds over time.

The businesses we work with typically see measurable improvements within the first two months after launch.
author: Hazelgrouse Studio
metaTitle: How to Get Found on Google Maps: A Guide for Local Businesses | Hazelgrouse Studio
metaDescription: Google Maps is where local customers look first. Learn what determines local pack rankings and how Polish businesses can improve their visibility on Google Maps.
